narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, an LSAT score of 26, middle-family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 2 and 1, respectively. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated an 82% chance you'll fail the bar on your first try— a risk level that led to your application's rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out the most compared to typical applicants. The strongest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier relative to peers who typically pass the bar. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 26, significantly lower than the dataset average of 36.7, which the model sees as a major hurdle since higher scores strongly correlate with bar success in past students. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, slightly below the average of 3.21, actually worked a bit in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, showing some resilience despite other challenges. Overall, your profile ranks lower academically than most admitted students who go on to pass, especially in those later-year grades and standardized testing where averages hover much higher. While factors like your full-time plans and middle-income background align with the majority (over 90% full-time), they couldn't offset the top concerns. This doesn't define your potential—many in similar situations, including Hispanic students, have succeeded by targeting weak spots. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward 35 or higher, strengthening your later-year academics through targeted courses or tutoring, and exploring post-bac programs to lift your overall record. Reapply after these improvements, perhaps with added work experience or community leadership to showcase your determination. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
